perturbation.original_text: In the case of unauthorised disclosure and/or loss of Confidential Information, the Receiving Party must immediately inform the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of possible unauthorized disclosure or loss of Confidential Information, the Receiving Party should consider informing the Disclosing Party when practical.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates immediate notification of unauthorized disclosure or loss. The modified text changes 'must' to 'should consider', making notification discretionary. Also, the phrase 'when practical' introduces ambiguity regarding the timing and circumstances of notification, weakening the obligation to report data breaches promptly and effectively.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 33 of the GDPR mandates that a data controller must notify the relevant supervisory authority of a personal data breach without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it. The modified text's weakening of the notification requirement introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with this GDPR provision, as it allows for delayed or omitted notification, potentially exceeding the 72-hour limit or failing to notify at all.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 33 GDPR – Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55 , unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. 2 Where the notification to the supervisory authority is not made within 72 hours, it shall be accompanied by reasons for the delay. The processor shall notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. The notification referred to in paragraph 1 shall at least: describe the nature of the personal data breach including where possible, the categories and approximate number of data subjects concerned and the categories and approximate number of personal data records concerned; communicate the name and contact details of the data protection officer or other contact point where more information can be obtained; describe the likely consequences of the personal data breach; describe the measures taken or proposed to be taken by the controller to address the personal data breach, including, where appropriate, measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects. Where, and in so far as, it is not possible to provide the information at the same time, the information may be provided in phases without undue further delay. 1 The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken. 2 That documentation shall enable the supervisory authority to verify compliance with this Article. Suitable Recitals ( 85 ) Notification Obligation of Breaches to the Supervisory Authority ( 87 ) Promp
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the mandatory notification requirement of a data breach, creating a contradiction with Article 33 of the GDPR. The original text uses 'must immediately inform,' while the changed text uses 'should consider informing... when practical,' making the notification discretionary and potentially delaying it. Article 33 of the GDPR, as stated in 'Scraped Law Snippet 2,' mandates that 'the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority.' The changed text introduces ambiguity and allows for potential non-compliance with this GDPR provision by not mandating immediate notification.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party undertakes to take all possible measures that are necessary to prevent and avoid disclosure and/or loss of any Confidential Information. In any case, such measures should comply with the measures that the Receiving Party would take for proper handling and protection of its own confidential information, and thus it must be the safety level that is not less than may reasonably be necessary.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will endeavor to implement security measures they deem adequate to safeguard Confidential Information. In considering appropriate measures, the Receiving Party will have regard to their existing security practices and what is reasonably necessary in their judgment.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Replacing 'undertakes to take all possible measures that are necessary' with 'will endeavor to implement security measures they deem adequate' introduces subjectivity and reduces the required level of protection. The phrase 'reasonably necessary in their judgment' gives the Receiving Party broad discretion, potentially leading to inadequate security and contradicting data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information to protect the personal information. The modified text permits the Receiving Party to determine 'adequate' and 'reasonably necessary' security measures based on their own judgment, which may fall below the 'reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information' standard required by CCPA, leading to potential non-compliance.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correct : You may ask businesses to correct inaccurate information that they have about you. Right to limit use and disclosure of sensitive personal information : You can direct businesses to only use your sensitive personal information (for example, your social security number, financial account information, your precise geolocation data, or your genetic data) for limited purposes, such as providing you with the services you requested. You also have the right to be notified, before or at the point businesses collect your personal information, of the types of personal information they are collecting and what they may do with that information. Generally, businesses cannot discriminate against you for exercising your rights under the CCPA. Businesses cannot make you waive these rights, and any contract provision that says you waive these rights is unenforceable. 2. What if I am not a California resident? Only California residents have rights under the CCPA. A California resident is a natural person (as opposed to a corporation or other business entity) who resides in California, even if the person is temporarily outside of the state. 3. What is considered personal information and sensitive personal information under the CCPA? Personal
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, which contradicts the intent of California Civil Code 1798.81.5(a)(1) to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. The original text required 'all possible measures that are necessary,' while the changed text allows the Receiving Party to implement security measures they deem 'adequate' and 'reasonably necessary in their judgment.' This introduces subjectivity and a lower standard of care. Snippet 1 (CIV 1798.81.5(b)) states that a business 'shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information,' which the modified text arguably undermines by allowing the Receiving Party's judgment to dictate what is 'adequate,' potentially falling below the 'reasonable security procedures and practices' standard. The changed text gives broad discretion to the Receiving Party, potentially leading to inadequate security and non-compliance with CCPA requirements.

perturbation.original_text: All disputes which may arise from or in connection with this Agreement shall be settled through negotiations between the Parties. If such a dispute between the Parties cannot be settled through negotiations between the Parties, either Party may submit such a dispute to a court in accordance with [ specify the jurisdiction ] laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising from this Agreement may be discussed informally between the Parties. Should an amicable resolution not be achieved, either Party might consider pursuing legal remedies within the general legal framework.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates mandatory negotiation followed by the option to submit disputes to court. The modified text replaces 'shall be settled through negotiations' with 'may be discussed informally,' making negotiation optional. Furthermore, replacing 'either Party may submit such a dispute to a court' with 'either Party might consider pursuing legal remedies' introduces uncertainty and discourages formal legal action. The phrase 'general legal framework' is vague. This undermines the enforceability of the agreement and could violate rules regarding dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-305 states that remedies provided by the UCC must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. By weakening the dispute resolution process and making it less certain that a party can pursue legal remedies, the modification undermines this principle and could limit the aggrieved party's ability to obtain adequate compensation for a breach of contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. (a) The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed but neither consequential or special damages nor penal damages may be had except as specifically provided in [the Uniform Commercial Code] or by other rule of law. (b) Any right or obligation declared by [the Uniform Commercial Code] is enforceable by action unless the provision declaring it specifies a different and limited effect. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the dispute resolution process, potentially limiting an aggrieved party's ability to obtain adequate compensation, thus contradicting UCC § 1-305. The original text mandates negotiation and allows court submission, whereas the modified text makes negotiation optional and legal action uncertain. This directly undermines the UCC's aim to put the aggrieved party in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed."

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party undertakes to promptly return to the Disclosing Party, or, at the Disclosing Party’s discretion, destroy all the Confidential Information (together with all copies and extracts) stored by the Receiving Party and/or its representatives (including without limitation, on computer disks and/or other digital media). Within five (5) calendar days from the date of termination (expiration) of this Agreement, unless there was a written request from the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall destroy all Confidential Information (together with all copies and extracts) stored by the Receiving Party and/or its representatives (including without limitation, on computer disks and/or other digital media). In case of destruction of Confidential Information under the terms of this Section, the Receiving Party shall send to the Disclosing Party a written confirmation of such destruction, which should contain a list of the destroyed Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will use commercially reasonable efforts to return or destroy Confidential Information. Following termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may, at its convenience, destroy Confidential Information, and providing confirmation of destruction is at their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates prompt return or destruction of confidential information upon request and specifies a 5-day deadline after termination, along with a mandatory confirmation of destruction. The modified text weakens these obligations by introducing 'reasonable request', 'commercially reasonable efforts,' and 'at its convenience,' making compliance less certain. Furthermore, making confirmation of destruction discretionary removes a key verification measure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. While not explicitly mandating destruction of trade secrets, courts often consider the measures taken to protect trade secrets, including return or destruction of confidential information, when assessing damages and injunctive relief. By weakening the obligations to return or destroy confidential information and making verification discretionary, the modification could undermine the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce their trade secret rights under the DTSA, as it suggests a lack of reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of their trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities by using terms like 'reasonable request' and 'commercially reasonable efforts,' which weakens the original obligation to promptly return or destroy confidential information. This ambiguity undermines the protection of trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1836, focuses on civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation and doesn't explicitly mandate specific actions like destruction, the changes in the contract weaken measures to protect the confidentiality, potentially affecting the ability to enforce trade secret rights under the DTSA. The modification, by making the confirmation of destruction discretionary, further reduces accountability and control over confidential information, increasing the risk of misappropriation.
